data.title: First American Financial: Strong Earnings Power Ahead Of A Housing Recovery
data.description:
There are stocks that look expensive because the market believes in their story, and then there are stocks that look cheap because the market is looking the other way. First American Financial Corporation (FAF), which I last covered in October 2023, sits firmly in the second category today.

With housing activity still subdued and mortgage rates stubbornly elevated, sentiment around the title insurance industry remains muted. But beneath that surface, First American is building operating leverage, investing in technology, and positioning itself for a powerful earnings inflection when the cycle turns.

This is not just a recovery story. It is a combination of cyclical upside, structural efficiency gains, and a surprisingly durable earnings base that the market may be underestimating.

The Business: More Than Just Title Insurance

First American is best known as one of the largest providers of title insurance and settlement services in the United States. At its core, the company ensures that property ownership is legitimate and that transactions close smoothly. That makes it deeply tied to real estate transaction volumes, both residential and commercial.

But reducing First American to a pure housing proxy misses key parts of the story. The company has built a broader ecosystem that includes data solutions and a growing banking platform through First American Trust. It also has technology initiatives designed to modernize how real estate transactions are executed.

Recent developments highlight that evolution. Management has leaned heavily into AI and automation, rolling out internal platforms that reduce processing times and expand quality control capacity.

At the same time, strategic initiatives like Endpoint aim to digitize and standardize the closing process across its network. These are not incremental tweaks. They are foundational changes that could reshape cost structure and scalability over time.

Earnings Strength: Cyclical Headwinds, Operational Tailwinds

First American’s latest Q1'26 earnings showed just how much progress is happening beneath the surface. Adjusted EPS came in at $1.33, up 58% YoY, a striking result given the still-muted housing backdrop.

The biggest driver was commercial strength. Revenue in that segment surged 48%, with record first-quarter performance. Large transactions are coming back, and the company is benefiting from strong activity across asset classes. Notably, data centers emerged as a major tailwind, with DC-related revenue up 76%. Energy-related transactions also surged, growing 250% and becoming a top-five asset class for the quarter.

Residential, by contrast, remains weak. Purchase revenue declined 4% from the prior year, validating management’s continued cautious stance on the housing market. Refinance activity provided only a temporary boost when mortgage rates dipped, and volumes have since softened again.

What stands out is how well the company performed despite those residential headwinds. That speaks to diversification within commercial and the growing contribution from its banking segment.

Notably, First American Trust generated steady investment income, supported by deposit growth of 19% YoY to $6.8 billion. Importantly, nearly a third of those deposits now come from outside the company’s traditional title business, signaling a more durable and less cyclical funding base.

Forward Outlook: Positioned for a Multi-Layered Recovery

Looking ahead, the investment case hinges on both cyclical recovery and structural growth. On the cyclical side, the housing market does not need to fully normalize for First American to benefit. Even a modest increase in transaction volumes, particularly in residential purchase activity, would provide meaningful operating leverage given the company’s cost structure.

Industry trends support that eventual recovery. Demographic demand remains strong, with millennials continuing to move into prime homebuying years. At the same time, supply constraints have limited transaction volumes rather than underlying demand. When mortgage rates stabilize or decline, activity could rebound quickly.

Commercial real estate adds another layer. While some sectors remain challenged, areas like data centers and energy infrastructure are seeing robust investment. First American’s recent results show it is well positioned to capture that activity.

The banking segment also provides a steady tailwind. As deposit sources diversify and expand, the contribution from investment income should grow, smoothing earnings across cycles.

One of the more underappreciated drivers of future earnings is the company’s aggressive push into AI and workflow automation.

Management highlighted several tangible benefits already in place. AI-assisted examination tools are reducing processing time by roughly 30 minutes per file. In the agency division, automation has expanded quality control capacity more than sixfold.

Endpoint, the company’s digital closing platform, is another key piece. Early pilots show roughly 30% of closing tasks can already be automated. As adoption expands across the branch network, management expects meaningful gains in both efficiency and customer experience.

These improvements matter because title insurance has historically been a labor-intensive business. If First American can structurally reduce time per transaction while maintaining accuracy, the incremental margin on higher volumes could be significant when the market recovers.

Financial Position, Dividends, and Valuation

First American operates from a position of financial strength. It has a BBB- rated balance sheet that’s supported by a safe 32% debt-to-capital ratio.

Capital allocation has remained disciplined. The company has historically returned capital through dividends and share repurchases while continuing to invest in strategic initiatives. As shown below, FAF opportunistically bought back shares a few years ago when shares were much cheaper.

FAF currently yields a respectable 3.2%. The dividend comes with a 5-year CAGR of 4% and 15 consecutive years of growth. I continue to see value in the stock at the current price of $68.84 with a forward P/E of 10.6, sitting well under its normal P/E of 14.9, as shown below.

Given its robust momentum across sectors and analyst estimates for 7-9% EPS growth annually over the next two years, investors could see double-digit total returns. This is when also factoring in dividends and reversion to mean valuation.

Risks to Consider

The most immediate risk is the housing market itself. If mortgage rates remain elevated for longer than expected, residential transaction volumes could stay depressed, limiting near-term earnings growth.

Commercial real estate also carries uncertainty. While some sectors are sound, others, particularly office, remain under pressure. A broader slowdown in commercial transactions would weigh on results.

Finally, title insurance is a competitive industry with pricing pressure during downturns. Maintaining margins in a low-volume environment requires careful cost control and operational discipline.

Investor Takeaway

First American Financial is not a flashy story, and that is precisely what makes it interesting. The market is focused on current housing weakness, but the company is building a stronger, more scalable business underneath.

Earnings are already showing resilience, driven by commercial strength, banking contributions, and early benefits from technology investments. When residential activity eventually recovers, those same factors could amplify the upside.

At today’s valuation, investors are effectively paying for a subdued housing environment. Meanwhile, they are getting the potential for margin expansion, deposit-driven earnings stability, and a cyclical rebound as a free option. As such, I maintain a ‘Buy’ rating on FAF.

data.title: Get Out Of Super Micro Computer While You Can
data.description:
Super Micro Computer (SMCI) is down over 11.5% on Thursday renewed negative headlines.

We're seeing emerging reports around the company's contract with Oracle (ORCL) for Nvidia’s (NVDA) GB300 NVL72 racks. According to Bluefin research, Oracle canceled a contract estimated at $1.1 billion to $1.4 billion following the indictment in late March. Bluefin research also raised concerns around the B200 inventory that SMCI has, and called it “considerable,” with demand shifting to GB200 NVL72 and racks awarded to competitors. The concern that arises here is that it may grow increasingly difficult for SMCI to get this inventory off its hands, considering that in the second half of the year, Nvidia’s Vera Rubin will be everyone's focus.

Before I get into the exhaustive details, when I last covered the stock following the scandal, shedding over 33% in a single day, I saw a buying opportunity. As a matter of fact, I upgraded the stock to a strong buy as I saw that the negatives were priced in at the time.

With the stock reaching the $29 level on Wednesday, my expectations have played out, even better than expected. The stock is now up over 30% since my strong buy, and I’m downgrading it to a sell until further notice, especially given the renewed uncertainty the report introduces.

My longer-term thesis remains, but I’m also not underestimating the market’s tendency to punish this name twice for the same mistake, especially when the second leg of the story introduces structural cost increases, and uncertainty around customers.

Why the risk-reward turned unfavourable:

The original SMCI sell-off treated the Liaw indictment as an event, something that got quantified and could be digested. A single-day 33% drop is the market's way of saying let's move on, and that's exactly the logic I followed when I upgraded to strong buy.

But if you layer the news from Thursday on top of SMCI's existing credibility deficit, including the Hindenburg report, the EY resignation, the delayed 10-K, the Nasdaq compliance scare, the ongoing DOJ interest, what you have is a governance overhang, which will show up on the numbers with Oracle’s cancellation. Every time the market was ready to let the latter’s discount compress, something new has emerged to justify it.

My original thesis, which is that fundamentals and AI server demand would overwhelm the scandal impact, has largely played out in the 30%+ move since my upgrade, but the risk-reward scenario from current levels looks increasingly unfavourable.

Fundamental risks:

I'm not saying SMCI is a broken story, and I want to make that clear. The AI server TAM is enormous, and SMCI's engineering capability is real. When the risk-reward was skewed positive after an overdone sell-off, I took the trade. I’m recommending investors take the profit and wait.

Market share risks are also a point of concern. On the latter, one must also consider competition. Back in 2024, according to estimates, the company had 40% to 50% market share in the AI GPU server deployments, down from around 80% in 2022.

SMCI previously held around 7.4% of global server OEM sales and now sits closer to 4%, nearly halving its footprint in the broader server market at the exact moment the company is being marketed as an AI infrastructure leader.

The top five server companies collectively saw their combined share decline from 33.3% to 22.6%, as Asian OEMs like undercut Western pricing with lower-cost manufacturing footprints, while hyperscalers simultaneously push internal silicon programs like Amazon’s (AMZN) AWS Trainium and Inferentia, Google's (GOOG) TPU, and Microsoft's (MSFT) Maia to reduce external OEM dependence over time. SMCI is the second-largest OEM seller of AI-aligned servers, but with roughly 63% of quarterly revenue coming from a single large data center customer, and over 90% of revenue tied to AI GPU platforms, the situation is now far from uncomplicated.

SMCI is losing share in the broader OEM market, and depending on one customer for nearly two-thirds of its revenue, all while that customer and its peers are spending serious capital to make SMCI less relevant to their infrastructure stack over the medium term. One other big point of concern is 2Q26 gross margin coming in at 6.4% non-GAAP, down from 9.5% in 1Q and double-digits in the 2024 peak era.

According to the company’s CEO, Charles Liang, on the last earnings call:

the customer mix, we are improving quarter after quarter. Now we have many more large-scale customer, I would like to say. So that will improve our profitability. The other factor is -- last quarter, I mean, December quarter, the GP300 was a little bit new to us. So a lot of expedite transportation cost. And now, I mean, product is getting mature. So those expedite transportation costs will be dramatically reduced and tariff impact also improving. And -- so overall, especially DCBBS also increasing for our -- for our gross margin. So I believe our gross margin will start to improve quarter after quarter.

I think a lot rests on margins, and that’s a risk investors should keep an eye on going forward.

Valuation and what’s next:

The stock is set to open at around $26, down from $29 at Wednesday’s close. If the losses don’t deepen, the stock would still be holding above its EMA21 and EMA50, but still below the $35 level where the EMA200 sits and is acting as resistance. The stock has been on a downward trend since early October of last year, and has seen a handful of good days since, reaching a bottom of $19.4 in late March. While it has recovered since, it is nowhere near the top, and is actually closer to the bottom at these levels. Following the Oracle report, I expect the stock to price in more of the negatives, especially if others follow Oracle’s footsteps, with the latter down around 3% on the news alone.

In comparison to DELL and HPE, SMCI is the cheapest on forward PE, forward EV/Sales, and almost every other forward metric.

While that might look attractive in retrospect, I expect some more weakness to get priced into the stock in the near term, and thus see the exit at current levels as the next best move before getting a better entry point.

data.title: Layoffs Are Accelerating Again
data.description:
It's been a crazy week for layoff announcements. Big Tech firms are continuing to clear out their rosters.

Amazon (AMZN) is closing a warehouse that opened less than two years ago only a few months after having laid off ~30,000 workers; Meta Platforms (META) is cutting its staff by 10%, which translates to ~8,000 employees; and Microsoft (MSFT) is offering buyouts to 7% of its U.S. workforce. This comes after other tech firms like Oracle (ORCL) cut thousands of jobs last month.

It's not just tech firms; Nike (NKE) is laying off ~1,400 workers too. But the workers laid off from Nike were all in technology-related roles in the operations division, so even these are ultimately tech layoffs.

A popular online tracker of tech sector layoff announcements, Layoffs.fyi, is now showing ~92,270 layoffs from tech companies alone so far in 2026. This puts us well ahead of schedule to blow past the 2025 end figure of 124,201, which is alarming in itself. Don't let the global nature of the figures fool you; 2/3rds of those layoffs are from the U.S.

AI Is the Cause and Effect

In early February, I wrote about an alarming print from the Challenger Report, which tracks layoff announcements, which I had written off as being idiosyncratically related to UPS (UPS) and Amazon (AMZN) reducing their transportation divisions. It highlighted that while large companies still don't employ the majority of Americans, they do employ such a large number of people that single cuts can trigger massive movements in absolute terms.

Since January, the layoff figure has moderated, but we'll get an update on this in a week and a half with April's figures, and I expect it to tick back up again due to all of these recent announcements. Challenger noted that tech layoff plans were accelerating, up 40% YoY, led by layoffs at Dell (DELL).

Challenger Report History

I covered what I think the reason is in a recent article titled “AI Already Took 3 Million Jobs,” where I presented a counterfactual for the AI jobs narrative. If we looked at the pre-2019 trend in knowledge worker jobs, where would we be if there was no ChatGPT?

Here's from that article, relevant to why these layoffs are accelerating:

Enter the counterfactual, an index constructed of white-collar sectors at threat from AI tools: finance, insurance, information, professional services, and business services jobs as reported by the census. We can abbreviate it “FIIPB” for our purposes. That group accounts for 40% of GDP and has historically shown a fairly linear growth rate; it was steady from 2015 to 2019 at ~1.9% annual growth. When we extend the trend and look at where we are now, the counterfactual shows us that we're off-trend by ~8%. That's 3 million jobs.

Two-thirds of that loss is in professional and business services, which didn't see the same steepness as information jobs but account for the majority of the off-trend data. This checks out to me—AI replaces much of what you may have been hiring for in these fields, like consultants...

And this is backed up by what's going on in the boardroom. In Q1 this year, CEOs spent a lot of time discussing AI; it was the most emphasized keyword across the 12,000 earnings calls that IoT Analytics tracked. They can't get enough of it, from the topic itself to related keywords like agentic and physical AI, Claude, Gemini, and ChatGPT—they all made it too, and even technical AI terms like “MCP” got onto the board for Q1.

While I do suspect that there are corporations using AI as cover to mask other reasons for layoffs that are far less flattering for their image, there is significant reason to believe that there is a trend in job destruction in both future and present jobs in the knowledge sector.

All of this is showing up in unemployment data. While the headline BLS figure is falling, that's been in part due to workers leaving the labor force and becoming “marginally attached.” Broader views of unemployment that include those workers and those underemployed (working part-time but seeking full-time work), like Morning Consult's unemployment index, show that levels are rising and above their YoY levels.

It's Not All Gloomy Labor Data

Despite all of this, I have a somewhat rosy view of the direction of the labor market. I believe that we're over the big labor hump and that these tech layoffs are concentrated among IT workers and that those workers are being absorbed quickly. We're still cooling, and I'm not saying that the labor market is improving. But I believe that we're over the worst of the tight labor conditions.

Initial jobless claims have come in under expectations and well below their YoY figure so far this year. There are ~20,000 fewer starting claims than there were this time last year.

Private sector employment is still growing despite the layoffs. ADP Research ((ADP)) is showing a positive uptick in employment ahead of the lagging BLS data. If ADP is correct in its directionality, this would constitute more confidence than the media headlines are projecting for the labor market.

Since the release of agentic coding tools last summer, we've also seen a structural rise in business application filings. So some job loss is being absorbed by the fact that entrepreneurship comes with lower barriers to entry.

As expected in an AI-fueled entrepreneurship boom, the fastest-growing sector is professional services. This is exactly where AI being able to do knowledge work is useful, in replacing salaried workers in both large and small businesses. That comes with both layoffs and new business ventures.

Will This Move The Fed to Act?

The labor market has been the key reason for the Fed to cut rates this year, and the unemployment rate falling from its peak (alongside the rise in inflation coming from fuel prices), so this mixed outlook rightfully has made them fall back into the “wait and see” mode that they took for much of last year. You can see it in the rate chart; long periods of holding rates go by between sets of rate cuts more.

The Fed is calling this prudence, though other commentators are calling it reactionary and too slow.

The current outlook is that while the labor market remains a mixed bag with specific pockets of pain (young graduates, coders, etc.), inflation is still a concern and over target, so the Fed will be in “wait and see” mode for the foreseeable future.

The market has priced in the Fed holding rates through all of this year and next, with the first cut currently projected to come in December 2027.

I think that this is rather dramatic and that the odds are likely to change quickly if new data hits of a worsening of labor market conditions. Not only am I still expecting the unemployment rate to moderate and level off soon, I expect that to motivate the Fed, once the energy shock has worked its way through in the coming months, to cut rates again toward the end of this year.

A lot can happen between now and then. My September timeline is absolutely in jeopardy, and traders are currently pricing in an 80% chance of rates being held at the December meeting this year. I'd bet otherwise, and I'd bet on one cut lower by then.

Conclusion

Recent layoff announcements have raised rightful alarms in labor market watchers, especially those looking at jobs in the tech sector. There is evidence to suggest that AI is replacing knowledge workers, in what CEOs are saying to cover for layoffs and in their earnings calls, as well as in the labor data for knowledge workers at large.

Despite this, I believe that the total picture of the labor market is looking like it is no longer worsening. Headline unemployment is falling, despite marginal attachment rising. But other metrics like initial jobless claims are coming in under expectations and lower than previous years, private payroll projections are showing near-term growth in hiring, and business formation in sectors benefiting from AI (potentially led by those tech workers recently laid off going in on their own projects) are rising faster and looking structurally higher than in the past.

All told, this puts the Fed in a bind for their prior rate cut plans. Their new plan to wait and see, which traders expect to last for the foreseeable future, hinges on the worst of labor market pain being behind us. I share that view, although I expect them to find cover to cut rates sooner than the market consensus, perhaps by the end of this year.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: 2 BDCs To Sell Before It Gets Worse
data.description:
The BDC sector has been hammered hard from multiple sides.

It is the cockroaches, SaaSpocalopyse, capped private BDC redemptions, and several other themes that have all contributed to heavy discounts across the board.

As I have laid out in numerous previous articles, there is a very good chance that all these headlines are just creating noise and seeding unjustified panic.

We could, of course, argue about BDC fundamentals deteriorating on the margin, but not about the current discounts really materializing.

Just take a look at my recent piece, BDCs: The Time To Buy This 14% Yielding Sector Is Now, in which I have contextualized the prevailing discounts to a) most recent BDC financial data on the ground and b) historical credit shock events, which triggered a surge in non-accruals.

The conclusions are clear: at least based on Q4 2025 data points, BDCs show no signs of system-wide deterioration, and, just to put the recent non-accruals in perspective, they would have to increase by ~10x from Q4 levels, and the bankruptcies would have to land at a rate that is double of what we experienced in the COVID-19 period for the sector average P/NAV to become justified.

So, with this kind of backdrop in mind and with an increased bias towards high-quality BDCs, I have entered into a full BDC buying mode. Namely, all my incremental portfolio contributions go to BDCs until their portfolio's current income weight reaches close to 20% or until the discounts are halved (whichever is first).

Having said that, let me once again underscore the second aspect that I mentioned above, i.e., increased bias towards high-quality BDCs.

It is simple. It means two things.

First: going long prudent capital allocators, which typically are internally managed BDCs with conservative balance sheets and high margins of safety on the loan investment underwriting front. These would be the likes of Capital Southwest (CSWC) and Trinity Capital (TRIN).

Second: avoiding optically interesting BDCs that present deep-value opportunities (high yields, steep discounts), but which, in practice, don't meet all of my defensive BDC criteria (e.g., below average PIK, debt-to-equity below 1.2x, interest coverage above 2.0x, etc.).

The second part is easier said than done.

Believe me, I can't even count the number of times I have caught myself thinking about dipping my toes into the deep-value BDC land to potentially capture the benefit of unfairly discounted valuations (i.e., above-average yields and enhanced upside potential).

Of course, this "deep-value BDC land" is not even, and there are certainly some picks that carry much better risk-adjusted return profiles than others. Yet such cases are very rare. Most of them are likely pure yield traps.

Let me elaborate on two BDCs, which I view as pure "yield traps" that are likely to disappoint investors even further.

#1: FSK

FS KKR Capital Corp (FSK) has been among one of the worst-performing BDCs in the past year or so.

On a TTM basis, FSK's share price has contracted by ~42%. While there has been an NAV erosion involved in the process as well, the price drop has far exceeded the NAV loss.

As a result of this, we have, theoretically, an interesting buying opportunity. We can buy FSK almost 50% below NAV and get a forward yield of ~17.8%, which is based on the already cut quarterly base dividend level (from $0.64 per share to $0.45 per share).

On top of that, Q4 2025 adjusted NII per share is high enough to cover the current base dividend and even leaves ~15% of cushion in place that could be used to absorb future shocks or to grow the portfolio.

So, we could definitely make a decent case on FSK's outperformance if the BDC bearish sentiment fades and no tangible (fundamental) headwinds occur.

However, two things:

a) Hoping for a recovery scenario is great and actually something that I believe in, but we have to be humble and appreciate the odds of that not happening for quite some time. Plus, I think that it would be only prudent that we viewed BDCs with a degree of skepticism (i.e., assuming slight deterioration in the fundamentals from the sector-wide challenges) .

b) Based on Q4 2025 financial data, it is quite clear that FSK's earnings and NAV will very likely come under big pressure.

Let me share 5 reasons why I think FSK is headed towards additional stress.

First, FSK has only ~57% of its loan book linked to first-lien loans, which is the safest area to operate as a BDC. The sector average is 77%. Considering the merit of enhanced defense, the fact that FSK is so exposed to riskier loans does not come in handy, to say the least.

Second, FSK has a debt-loaded balance sheet. The debt-to-equity stands at 1.30x, which exceeds the sector benchmark of 1.25x. From this level, it is very difficult to grow a portfolio, as the benefits of a larger loan book get exponentially offset by the assumption of greater financial risk.

Third, speaking of leverage, relatively recently FSK got downgraded from investment grade to a junk BDC. It seems that FSK lenders are seeing the same risks as I do (and as the market does, which is implied by the heavy discount). The practical issue here is that it will render FSK's cost of debt much higher (and at the wrong time). By wrong time I'm referring to the fact that FSK will have to roll over $1 billion of 3.4% yielding debt at a much higher rate. In 2027 the issue is even worse. All this will inevitably put pressure on the NII per share generation levels.

Fourth, almost 16% of FSK's total income stems from PIK, which is materially above the sector average of 8.5%. There are two issues from this: 1) if we express the base dividend coverage on a cash basis, we will arrive at a slightly underfunded base dividend level (not ~115%, that is, if we factor in PIK), and 2) PIK itself is, per definition, a higher-risk income source, where the relevant loans might eventually end up in a non-accrual status.

Fifth, FSK has been dealing with painful non-accruals for the past couple of quarters. As of Q4 2025, FSK had 5.5% of the portfolio's cost linked to non-accruals, which is also above the sector average of 3.5%. It does not send the right message about the portfolio's resiliency, which we would definitely require considering the four points above and the challenging sector environment.

In a nutshell, while the offer presented by FSK looks attractive from the P/NAV and yield perspective, the risks are simply too high to go long.

#2: TPVG

TriplePoint Venture Growth (TPVG) has a similar story to that of FSK.

On the one hand, the offering looks enticing.

The BDC trades at ~40% below NAV, distributes ~16% yielding dividend, and has a well-covered base dividend of 109%. Plus, in H1 2025, TPVG started paying lower dividends, which should theoretically indicate that the current yield is in a much greater position than before (e.g., when the cut was made, the management probably incorporated a buffer in order to avoid revisiting the payout again if some additional challenges arise).

On the other hand, the risks are way too high, exceeding the benefits that might come from the attractive discount and yield angle.

Let me explain.

It all starts with unfavorable leverage, which is itself a risk, but, worse, which magnifies the potential consequences of the other factors that I will be highlighting here.

TPVG has a debt-to-equity of 1.32x. A level that would require some de-risking, especially given TPVG's presence in the inherently risky BDC segment - i.e., venture growth funding.

What, in my view, is a potentially deadly combination is a) aggressive leverage and b) high concentration in increasingly challenged SaaS names, which have not yet established strong market shares and are in a phase where the prevailing EBITDA generation is negative or barely neutral.

TPVG embodies this combination.

As we can see in the pie chart below, at least 33% of the total loan book is tied to software names:

Obviously, we don't know how or whether the AI will really change the way SaaS creates value and generates sales going forward, but I wouldn't want to be sitting in holdings that have not established strong competitive moats and are dependent on external financing to make the cash flows work (i.e., venture growth stage SaaS firms).

The situation gets even worse if we add into the equation the PIK variable, which for TPVG is the highest in the entire BDC space - i.e., at ~21% of the total income. This nuance goes hand in hand with what I was saying above: that most venture-stage growth firms have very tight cash flow profiles, which increases their need to rely on PIK (non-cash-like item).

This leads me to the current base dividend coverage.

On the surface, the coverage stands at 109%, which is quite a decent level.

However, by expressing the ratio on a cash basis (which means carving out PIK), the real situation becomes very bad. Plus, just as FSK, TPVG will have to deal with unfavorable debt refinancing. In Q1 2026, TPVG had to refinance ~$200 million of 4.5% fixed-rate note, which accounted for almost 40% of its total outstanding borrowings. The new debt was attracted at a fixed rate of 7.5% and a much shorter tenure to keep the cost of debt as low as possible. Namely, the NII per share is likely to be pressured by increasing interest costs going forward.

In summary, there are too many things that could go wrong here, which don't make the overall investment case attractive enough, especially against such a backdrop, where extra prudence should be exercised.

data.title: Baker Hughes: Record Backlog, Iran Tailwinds, And AI Exposure Aren't Enough
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I last covered Baker Hughes (BKR) in December, I rated them Hold, highlighting their strong long-term potential but preferring to wait for a better entry point on the back of the near-term oil volatility that could’ve resulted in less demand/funds for the company’s services at the time.

Although BKR reported a strong quarter and stands to benefit from the Iran conflict (once resolved) in the near-term and their energy/AI/data center and LNG pivot in the long-term, their potential already seems to be priced in, while risks surrounding a weaker economy are rising the longer the current Middle East conflict lasts for.

Record Backlog Amid Portfolio Transformation

BKR reported a strong quarter, beating the market’s top- and bottom-line estimates while the $1.16 billion Adj. EBITDA and exceeded their guidance range alongside all others beating their midpoints, with very strong orders (up 26%, record backlog) and $210 million in FCF.

They also announced the divestiture of Waygate Technologies to Hexagon in a $1.45 billion all-cash deal complementing the Chart Industries acquisition and adjusting their portfolio, expected to close in H2 2026, with management stating that this would further streamlining their earnings and cash flow while strengthening the balance sheet. This is part of the company’s broader portfolio reshaping activity, expecting ~$3 billion in proceeds from divestments in 2026, with the PSI sale to Crane Company completed in Q1 for proceeds of $1.15 billion before closing adjustments.

For 2026, BKR expects their FCF conversion rate to approach 50% of the Adj. EBITDA (not including costs/interests associated with the Chart acquisition), although they also note that their current assumptions are that the Middle East disruptions continue throughout the end of June without further escalation, the Iran conflict resolves and the Strait of Hormuz is fully operational for the entire second half of the year.

Financially, based on BKR’s latest report, we continue to see a strong position, although the cash is inflated by the recent issuance for the pending Chart acquisition, yet they plan on reducing their leverage, as mentioned.

Regarding the all-cash acquisition of Chart, BKR has successfully priced a dual-currency offering that raised roughly $9.5 billion, with the $6.5 billion USD notes ranging from 4.05% to 5.85% interest (from 2029 to 2056) and the EUR ones coming at 3.226% to 4.737% (from 2030 to 2046), which is not terrible in this environment, but it also loads up their balance sheet at an uncertain time. Still, it's important to note that they target a de-leveraging phase within 24 months after the Chart deal closes, aiming at achieving a 1.0x to 1.5x net debt to LTM Adj. EBITDA, which would be sustainable, allowing them to focus on their dividends, flexible buybacks, and other investments.

Since the previous coverage, the company also announced several deals that highlight their importance regardless of the environment, marked by weak prices until March and war premiums after it, including a service contract with Petrobras (PBR), significant gas turbine order from data center projects in Georgia and Texas, forming a consortium for a major Syrian oil and gas project, backing HMH Holding in their recent IPO (raising ~$200 million as per their Q1 release), and partnering with Google Cloud (GOOG) on AI data center solutions - just to name some of their recent activity.

Iran’s a Double-Edged Sword

For Baker Hughes in particular, the Iran conflict degenerating is both a major risk and an opportunity, depending on how the situation unfolds.

To start with the negative part, not only are we talking about the risk of a global recession that would be terrible for their prospects since clients would invest less due to potentially lower oil and gas prices, but this can go as far as affecting the major AI investors, as Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and the UAE for example are massive in terms of global investments in the industry.

However, if we see a scenario where some degree of war premiums persist for longer - which seems very likely at this point - it’s also possible to see more demand for BKR’s services, as their clients would benefit from an influx of capital.

There’s also the possibility to see BKR benefit from the potential repairs in the region’s energy infrastructure, which may not be reflected in the market’s expectations just yet since it depends on how long the conflict lasts. As Qatar already said a few weeks into the conflict, Iran’s counter-attacks had already damaged its Ras Laf.. to the point where it would need 3 to 5 years of construction to get back to its previous levels, while the chief of the ECB warned a few weeks ago that the market is not yet fully understanding the damage done to the region’s strategic energy assets.

Plus, we should also take into account the catalyst coming from Venezuela’s potential emergence in the global oil market beyond what they were in the past, as we’re talking about the country with the largest oil reserves on the planet but also very obsolete infrastructure - which is amazing news for Baker Hughes, with companies like Exxon (XOM), Chevron (CVX) and others presenting interest in the region (again) especially after the Iran conflict escalated.

Meanwhile, the EIA now expects Brent prices at an average of about $88 by Q4 2026, which would fall further to $76 per barrel in 2027, indicating that even though fundamentals will likely take over again once the conflict de-escalates and the Strait of Hormuz is opened, we could still see a near-term boost in demand on top of lower supply due to the damage:

We now expect that Brent crude oil prices will increase from an average of $81/b in 1Q26 to a peak of $115/b in 2Q26 before gradually falling to an average of $88/b in 4Q26. We base this on the assumption that the conflict does not persist past April and that traffic through the Strait of Hormuz gradually resumes but does not return to pre-conflict levels until late 2026. Shut-in oil production gradually returns as flows through the strait resume and oil trade flows adjust. Given this relatively long adjustment period after flows through the strait resume, we expect oil prices will remain elevated, with Brent crude oil prices averaging $76/b in 2027, about $23/b higher than in our February STEO forecast.

As a note, we’re talking about significant increases since the institution’s previous estimates (and the conflict is still not over, meaning that there’s room for further revisions), which are also more and more in line with the IMF’s worse expectations, further emphasizing the rising pressure from a prolonged conflict on the global economy. As a note, there’s also potential to see these higher oil prices (if the EIA’s expectations come true) result in increased volumes for investments, which would also be great for BKR.

In the longer-term term however, we should also continue to see a boost for LNG, with a very comprehensive study from the National Center for Energy Analytics pointing out:

To meet that much new demand by 2030, underlying engineering realities show that most of the additional electricity generation will necessarily come from burning natural gas. That will, in turn, require about a 10% to 20% increase in overall U.S. gas production. That rise in gas demand will occur contemporaneously with roughly the same amount of new demand coming from additional LNG export terminals that are already under construction.

Risks Reiterated

Although BKR has great potential from the growing AI energy demands and potential to even gain from the damage done in the Middle East, we should also keep in mind that the environment can continue to deteriorate, potentially leading to a higher-for-longer rate environment or even a recession,

Meanwhile, there’s also risk coming from Baker Hughes’ internal moves, with the Chart acquisition increasing the possibility of seeing missteps or malinvesting, and I mentioned before how the company itself doesn’t have the best history of growth, while the significant levels of debt they issued for it can add pressure on their near-term.

Valuation

For BKR’s DCF to Equity valuation, starting from the company’s 2026 guidance, we should see about $2.4 billion in FCF, which I’ll assume will reach $2.8 billion in 2027 and $3.1 billion in 2028 thanks to the synergies and portfolio adjustments they’re working on for the Horizon 2 phase, which I’ll assume can grow at a 6% CAGR until 2030 on further improvements, and a 4% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years thanks to the strong potential coming from their future-facing portfolio especially if the economy improves, as well as a 3% terminal growth rate given their global exposure, expansion beyond oil services, and pricing power.

To take into account the macro risks and increased leverage, I’ll assume a 10% discount rate, with 996 million shares outstanding as of their Q1 report.

This gets us to an equity value of $44.44 billion, for an intrinsic value of $44.62 per share, which is below the current $63.81, implying that the market is likely willing to take a lower return now just to ensure they’re part of the company’s potential pivot towards AI/data centers and the potential post-Iran conflict repairs. For reference, using an 8% discount rate under the same assumptions gets us to an intrinsic value of $63.16, which is nearly identical to current levels, but I believe it’s too little to ask for in today’s environment.

Conclusions

BKR remains a Hold, as even though the company has great long-term potential and should be able to benefit from the Iran conflict’s subsequent repairs and a higher-for-longer oil environment, this potential seems to be priced in already, leaving little room for error and a margin of safety that’s not attractive enough.

data.title: Buy Fear And Sell Greed
data.description:
The stock market finally caught wind that oil prices keep climbing, which led to an abrupt sell-off around 1pm yesterday afternoon, but the dip buyers didn’t wait long before narrowing the loss into the close. Any assumption that the war in Iran is winding down is clearly misplaced, as Iran remains defiant, and there were reports as recently as yesterday that the IRGC is planting additional mines in the waterway. That led President Trump to publicly order the U.S. Navy to “shoot to kill” any small Iranian boats in the act. He also announced after the close that the ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon would be extended by three weeks, which might have some meaning if it ever started in the first place. The war is not over. Instead, it seems to be intensifying.

This is why oil prices have been creeping higher all week, with WTI crude touching $98 and Brent back to $106. Interest rates are also inching higher this week, with the 10-year Treasury yield back to 4.33% and the 2-year at 3.84%. The stock market is on a different path. After factoring in the geopolitical risks with a 10% correction in the S&P 500, the market is completely ignoring it now. We have swung from fear to greed at record pace, but the economic data is starting to diverge.

Yesterday, S&P Global released its mid-month survey of manufacturing and services companies (Flash Composite PMI) for April. The index rose to 52.0 from a downwardly revised 50.3 in March, with 50.0 being the line of demarcation between expansion and contraction. That was the good news, as the economy rebounded from near-stagnation last month following the outbreak of the war. The details were less inspiring.

The sub-index for the more dominant service sector rebounded from modest contraction to a reading of 51.3, with new business orders increasing at the slowest pace in two years amid a continued decline in exports. Respondents blamed uncertainty caused by the war alongside affordability issues. Surging prices and the prospect for higher borrowing costs also weighed on activity.

The manufacturing sector saw its sharpest rise in output in four years, with the sub-index rising to a 47-month high of 54.0, but there is a caveat. The improvement was attributed to a domestic “panic” to build inventories in advance of price hikes and supply shortages. Export orders continued to weaken. Additionally, input costs soared at a pace not seen since mid-2022, with average selling prices seeing the largest monthly increase since July 2022.

According to Chris Williamson, who is the Chief Economist at S&P Global, this PMI is consistent with a rate of economic growth just under 1%. Meanwhile, the stock market continues to make new highs in anticipation of better days to come. I think this divergence between market sentiment and economic fundamentals will close in the weeks ahead. There are clear signs of excessive exuberance after this three-week rally. According to Goldman Sachs, the highest-risk baskets of stocks are leading the way, with its Meme Stock basket surging 27.9% in April alone and its Non-Profitable Tech basket not far behind at 25.6%.

I think we have gone past recovering from the fear of the conflict to greed that is already excessive far too soon. I was buying March’s fear but gradually shedding risk as this rally ensued. Markets are overbought and need to consolidate on clear signs that the conflict is nearing its end and the Strait of Hormuz is reopening. The rhetoric has run its course.

data.title: XPeng: Near-Term Growth Pains Meet Solid, Diversified Prospects - Reiterate Buy
data.description:
I previously reiterated XPeng Inc. (NYSE:XPEV) as a Buy in January 2026, thanks to the near-term break even prospects.

In this article, I shall discuss why XPEV remains a Great Buy, thanks to the robust monetization avenues beyond EVs to flying cars, robotaxi, and humanoid robots.

XPEV Faces Numerous Headwinds & Tailwinds Entering FY2026

Since my last Buy rating, XPEV has unfortunately breached the established 2025 trading floor of $18s to trade sideways along the $15/$16s since February 2026, with a similar correction also observed in its Chinese automaker peers in varying degrees.

1. Changing Regulations Trigger Delivery Headwinds

Given the ongoing policy shift across tax exemption and trade-in subsidies from 2026 onwards, it is unsurprising that the robust EV sales in 2025 has given way to the softer YTD numbers.

The same has been observed in XPEV through the Q1'26 delivery numbers of 62,682 (-44.6% QoQ, October/November/December 2025 and -33.3% YoY), aside from NIO (NIO) at +98.3% YoY delivery growth and Li Auto (LI) at +3.2% YoY (January/February/March 2026/Q1'25).

Given the impacted delivery trends against some of its outperforming peers, it is unsurprising that XPEV has mostly traded sideways at the new lows of $15/$16s, as the market discounts its automotive prospects at a time of mixed EV demand.

2. FQ1'26 Preview

With XPEV set to report their FQ1'26 performance on May 18, 2026 (Pre-Market), I urge readers to temper their expectations entering the earnings call.

This is because the management has offered a notably lower total FQ1'26 revenue guidance of RMB 12.74B (-42.7% QoQ/-19.4% YoY), with it apparent that the softer domestic demand has yet to be well balanced by the supposedly robust international exports.

For reference, XPEV previously delivered 45K vehicles in overseas market (+96% YoY), comprising 10.4% (-1.6 points YoY) of its overall deliveries at 429.44K vehicles in FY2025 (+125% YoY).

Despite the relatively low volume ratio, readers may want to note that the management has highlighted that "revenue from overseas markets contributing over 15% of total revenue," with it lending credence to the overseas market's likely higher Average Selling Prices [ASP] and the consequently richer profit margins.

While the management has guided overseas deliveries "to double in 2026 year-over-year with international business contributing over 20% of total revenue," it remains to be seen how material the overseas deliveries may be in Q1'26, attributed to the seasonally weaker Spring Festival holidays in February 2026.

This is especially since XPEV is unlikely to replicate their positive FQ4'25 net income margin of 2.2% (+2.9 points QoQ/+10.8 YoY) in the upcoming FQ1'26 earnings call.

This is attributed to the likely to be impacted manufacturing/operating leverage from the lower volumes/utilization trends and the consequently lower vehicle margins.

This is especially since XPEV is expecting to launch 4 new models across large/compact sized vehicles in 2026, which is likely to trigger near-term noise in their manufacturing/promotional ramp up.

Readers must also note their robotics ambition, with the "aims for a monthly production target of over 1,000 units by the end of this year" naturally explaining their higher physical AI-related R&D expense guidance at RMB 7B in FY2026 (+55.5% YoY).

As a result of their ongoing EV and robotics investment cadence, along with the intensified international push and autonomous driving capabilities through VLA 2.0, I am of the opinion that XPEV is likely to report lumpy FY2026 bottom-line performance at best, or worse, deteriorating margins.

As a result, the stock is likely to underperform for a little longer, especially since they "expects to start large-scale ​production of its flying cars" in 2027, with the elevated capex trends likely to be a norm in the intermediate term.

3. Long-Term Growth Prospects

Even then, I am of the opinion that XPEV's multi-pronged growth initiatives across:

  • the new EV models targeting international expansion to the EU/APAC/EMEA/Latam regions, and
  • the autonomous efforts from their recently launched VLA 2.0 platform in March 2026,

are likely to trigger robust growth prospects, as they announce their ongoing public road testing in China and their upcoming trial operations for their robotaxi capabilities "later this year."

This is on top of the new SaaS monetization opportunity, with Volkswagen (VWAGY) set to be XPEV's "first customer for XPENG's VLA 2.0 in the Chinese market" and the "global deliveries of VLA 2.0 planned for 2027."

While nascent, XPEV has highlighted their robust growth tailwinds from the VLA 2.0 launch, as similarly observed in the sequentially higher March 2026 sales numbers of 27,415 vehicles (+79.6% MoM/-17.4% YoY).

The management also expects to replicate VLA 2.0's generalized autonomous capability in overseas markets where there may be "smaller roads and also country roads or uncharted territories," without relying on "any overseas actual road data."

Combined with "both robotaxi and humanoid robots represent $1 trillion to $10 trillion level global market opportunities" and "the market for physical AI is expected to surpass that of the automotive industry" over the next 5 to 10 years, I maintain my conviction surrounding XPEV's robust, multi-pronged growth prospects as they expand overseas.

XPEV Remains A Great Buy Here

On the one hand, XPEV has breached the uptrend support line established since the August 2024 bottom, with the stock currently retesting the support levels in the $16s at the time of writing.

With the stock now entering oversold territories across the low RSIs and trading volumes, there may be a potential for further volatility indeed, worsened by the somewhat elevated short interest ratio of 4.94%.

On the other hand, thanks to the steep stock price correction of -39.9% from the 52-week highs and the still promising consensus forward estimates, I am of the opinion that XPEV looks even more compelling at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 1.07x, down from the 1Y mean of 1.31x and the 5Y mean of 3.29x.

When comparing XPEV's discounted FWD EV/Sales valuations of 1.07x and the consensus revenue growth at a 3Y CAGR of +21.5% (between FY2025 revenues of $10.97B and the consensus FY2028 estimates of $19.72B) to its Chinese automaker peers, including:

  • BYD Company (BYDDF) at 0.94x/+11.8%,
  • Xiaomi (XIACY) (XIACF) at 1.29x/+14.5%,
  • LI at 0.38x/+14.9%,
  • NIO at 0.92x/+25.3%,

along with its US-based automaker peers:

  • General Motors (GM) at 0.95x/+1.3%,
  • Ford (F) at 1.08x/+1.8%,
  • Stellantis (STLA) at 0.24x/+3.7%, and
  • Tesla (TSLA) at 13.82x/+13.5% respectively,

it is apparent that XPEV remains extremely cheap here, thanks to the recent stock price moderation and the double digits, diversified revenue growth prospects.

While it remains to be seen when the automaker may achieve a consistent break-even and/or profitability cadence, I am of the opinion that the stock's discounted valuations already warrant a reiterated Buy rating for those with a longer investing trajectory.

This is especially since XPEV is likely to remain well capitalized to fund their growth ambitions, based on:

  • their cash position of RMB 47.7B on their balance sheet (+13.5% YoY) and
  • the rich Free Cash Flow generation at RMB 5.1B in FY2025 (+220% YoY, based on net cash provided by (used in) operating activities of RMB 8.25B and purchase of property, plant and equipment at RMB 3.15B).

Despite the likely to be immense capex undertaking and the likely to be mixed bottom-line performance, I urge reader to look beyond their intermediate term growth pains and focus on their long-term growth opportunities across EVs, autonomous SaaS, robotaxi, robotics, and flying car segments.

XPEV remains a long-term winner, worth buying at every dip, in my view.

data.title: PENN Entertainment: The Inflection Point Is Here
data.description:
In my last article on PENN Entertainment (PENN), I rated the stock as a cautious buy. The “cautious” is due to the challenges the company faces in the iGaming segment, debt, and other cyclicality of the industry. The “buy” came from the more solid part of the business, such as cash flow from physical casinos, and PENN’s projects, but mainly from the very asymmetric valuation (such as a P/B below 1x).

And well, just yesterday PENN's stock rose 16%. Since my last article, it is already a 21% gain. And this is not by chance; the market did not suddenly realize that there is value in PENN. The trigger was a very solid Q1 earnings release, which helps clarify the outlook for PENN’s near future.

Even with this re-rating, I still think PENN is worth a buy rating. The margin of safety became a bit tighter due to the increase in market cap, but at the same time, now it is possible to have fewer doubts regarding execution. Overall, the case became more robust.

PENN Q1 Earnings: Digital Meets Physical Projects

PENN managed to beat consensus estimates both on the top line and EPS. For revenue it was a slight beat of $30 million, while adj. EPS was a big surprise, as the market expected a negative figure, and PENN delivered $0.12.

The table below makes this a bit clearer. Starting with the physical side, we notice a considerable increase in revenue in the Midwest and in the West, driven by the ramp-up of some projects such as the M Resort’s new Hotel Tower. Note that this increase in revenue allowed this segment to have more operating leverage to increase EBITDAR by a few million. Even if the volume is relatively low, EBITDAR growth in the West was 17%.

But certainly the highlight was the digital division. This Interactive segment not only grew revenue significantly but is already at an EBITDAR of only -$10 million. In Q1 last year, this figure was -$89 million; that is, it was a very large YoY improvement. But more than that, even looking on a QoQ basis, this was already a huge reduction, because in Q4 2025 PENN delivered a loss of about -$29 million in Interactive.

This may seem bad if you do not follow PENN, since we are still seeing a large loss in this division; that is, it is still something that is destroying value instead of generating it. But that is looking at the “photo.” Looking at the “movie,” things get more interesting. Q1 is theoretically the inflection point of digital, because PENN was focusing on new strategies to reach breakeven in the division by the end of the year. With this better result, the likelihood that they will actually be able to stabilize this business in 2026 is high (although there is a headwind from the Alberta launch that should have an impact of -$20 million).

This Interactive segment highlight also appears if we look at the income statement. The top line grew 6.4%, with highlights for food, beverage, hotel, and other, which grew 18.8%. But note that while food, beverage, and other expenses grew more than 24%, gaming expenses declined. We also have to give credit to controlled SG&A, which grew 6% (less than revenue), but in this quarter, basically the growth in operating income of more than 120% is due to digital improving.

But this does not mean that digital is now the crown jewel or anything like that. At least, not in my view. I still think PENN’s physical segment has a lot of value. What management mentioned on the earnings call about the development of new projects illustrates this. The expectation is for four development projects to generate 15% (or more) cash-on-cash returns. Considering the cost is ~$800 million, we are talking about an incremental cash flow of ~$120 million/year that should mature in the coming years:

“As we've said previously, we anticipate our 4 development projects will generate 15% plus cash-on-cash returns on our aggregate project cost of $800 million, which is net of the $50 million contribution from the City of Aurora. Overall, increases in both visitation and spend per visit company-wide supported year-over-year theoretical revenue growth across all of our rated worth segments, representing the largest quarterly increase in 3 years for the Retail segment.”

Below it is possible to see the projects (some are coming in a few months, while others should only come in 2028).

With all this, PENN’s profile has changed a bit. Guidance improved slightly, with the retail segment EBITDAR expected at ~$1.9 billion and interactive adj. EBITDA at ~-$20 million (as mentioned, due to the Alberta Launch). The best part is that the capital structure is also improving, with expectations that in 2026 net leverage ends at 2x-2.4x.

PENN Stock: Still Cheap

PENN’s traditional debt is at ~$2.6 billion, and the market cap is at $2.30 billion. That is, something close to $4.9 billion of enterprise value (not including lease debt). With operating income (EBIT) reaching ~$100 million per quarter, excluding only depreciation and amortization, we already have something closer to $200 million per quarter (EBITDA). That is, PENN is already a company capable of generating a GAAP EBITDA close to $800 million per year. That is, a “traditional” EV-to-EBITDA of 6.2x.

Of course, with the expectation of an adj. EBITDAR of almost $2 billion in retail, this multiple would already go to less than 3x. But if we are going to use EBITDAR, the correct approach would be to use the higher EV (including lease), which would be more than $11 billion. Even so, it would give a multiple slightly below 6x.

Even with the stock rising, the P/B also remained low; it is still at only 1.0x.

These are rough calculations, but they already serve to give us a good sense that the capacity to generate value here exists and remains asymmetric.

For instance, with this more solid Q1, I believe PENN should be able to stabilize its business in the coming years, and I believe that new projects should add to EBITDA/cash flow. If multiples using these proxies of shareholder value (EBITDA/EBITDAR) are close to 5x, it is already possible to see a certain level of asymmetry. Think of it this way: 5x EBITDA means that if PENN distributed the entire amount to shareholders, it would be a 20% dividend yield. Of course, this will not happen, neither in the short term nor potentially in the long term, since we have to consider CapEx, debt, interest, some adjustments, and so on. But this serves to see the company’s potential in the long term. As cash flow generation stabilizes (and continues growing even if in low single digits), projects mature, digital stops burning cash, and much more cash flow starts to be available to shareholders.

Bottom Line: More Clarity Does Not Mean Certainty

In this bottom line, I think it is worth keeping a more cautious tone and remembering that PENN is not a simple case.

Yes, Q1 improved visibility for the next quarters; now it is easier to believe that digital will stop burning cash and so on.

But at the same time, we have to remember that even for digital, there are risks, such as execution risk itself, since we will still have some quarters of losses (including the Alberta Launch) this year, and if something goes wrong, the market may punish PENN stock (with a de-rating) due to some loss of confidence in execution. Besides that, both the core and digital are very delicate segments, highly regulated, and there can always be negative surprises along the way. For instance, if digital breakeven is in 2026, possibly in 2027 or 2028 we would already see a positive EBITDA, right? Well, not necessarily. If there is heavy regulation, state taxation on online betting, competition, or even some macroeconomic pressure (recession), digital may go back to losses or delay this “profit”.

And even though I see the physical segment as something to stabilize and bring more recurring cash flow, it can also be affected by these macroeconomic uncertainties, and since PENN has this whole complex structure of high costs, and long-term contracts with REITs and similar, in these scenarios the financials can become "uglier" again.

But anyway, I think the results showed a more interesting path for PENN in terms of how shareholder value can eventually be high; that is, this is a high-risk case, but also high reward in a scenario where “everything goes right.” That is why I will maintain my cautious buy rating for PENN stock.

data.title: Lockheed Martin: Q1 Reality Check And The Road To 2030
data.description:
Introduction

It's not been too long since I last covered defense giant Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT) here on Seeking Alpha, but of course a lot has happened in the meantime (i.e., since late January). In my last article, I gave a preview going into Q4 and full-year 2025 earnings and explained why I am not a seller of LMT stock despite the 30%+ rebound from recent lows. Although the war in Iran had not yet begun at that point, the military intervention in Venezuela in early January (and, of course, the bombing of Iranian nuclear sites in June 2025) were signs that the likelihood of such a scenario was increasing.

Considering Lockheed Martin released its Q1 2026 earnings today and results were apparently disappointing (LMT shares are down 5% at the time of writing), it's a good time to give an update on the company's fundamentals. Besides the quarterly results, I'll discuss if anything has changed with regard to the company's long-term outlook, against the backdrop the ongoing war in Iran and generally heightened geopolitical tensions. Finally, I'll give a brief update on valuation, considering LMT shares so far declined almost 25% from its recent – war-induced – all-time high almost $700.

Lockheed Martin: A Disappointing Quarter, But Long-Term Fundamentals Remain Intact

For Q1 2026, Lockheed Martin reported earnings per share (EPS) of $6.44, well below consensus of $6.69 and down 12% year-over-year. Sales of $18.0 billion were basically flat year-over-year, while analysts expected modest growth to the tune of 1%. As illustrated in the segment sales overview in Figure 1, year-over-year declines in sales in the Rotary and Mission Systems (RMS) and Aeronautics segment were offset by higher sales in the Missiles and Fire Control (MFC) and Space segments. Aeronautics, quite understandably remains the main contributor to sales although I maintain that Lockheed is indeed very well diversified. Of course, this applies only from an operational perspective, taking into account the risks associated with Lockheed's heavy reliance on a single entity, namely the U.S. Department of Defense (or, since September 2025, the Department of War).

The main reason for the year-over-year decline in EPS (despite share buybacks executed in 2025) was margin compression in all four segments, as highlighted in the mini-table included in Figure 2. That said, at least the margin compression in the MFC segment was insignificant, resulting in meaningful operating profit growth of 7.5% year-over-year. The other segments, however, were rather disappointing, in my view.

The margin contraction in Aeronautics was largely due to profit booking rate adjustments in the F-16 and C-130 programs, which were attributable to production performance, development, and delivery delays, among other reasons. That said, Lockheed reported a favorable profit adjustment on the F-35 program, but the year-over-year margin contraction by 1.3 percentage points nonetheless says a lot. F-35 deliveries were rather disappointing in Q1 as well, at just 32 units (down from 47 in Q1 2025). No F-16s were delivered in Q1, but government helicopter deliveries more than doubled year-over-year, to 19 units.

The margin declines in RMS and Space were also due to negative profit booking rate adjustments. The CH-53K and Seahawk programs were responsible for the weakness in the RMS segment, and the Space segment's profitability suffered from a difficult comparison with Q1 2025. A look at Q1 2024 and Q1 2023 segment results, however, shows that the "normal" operating profitability of the Space segment is quite close to 10% (p. 11, Q1 2024 earnings release). The 3.6 percentage point decline from the 11.8% achieved in the first quarter of 2025 is therefore most likely partly attributable to operational issues as well.

Taken together, and leaving the good performance in MFC aside, the year is off to a disappointing start for Lockheed, in my view. That said, and despite operational issues which I hope are resolved quickly, I remain positive in particular with regard to the Aeronautics segment's longer-term margin potential, largely stemming from the gradual growth in – higher-margin – recurring services and modernization revenues in the F-35 program.

However, despite these undoubtably quite sobering results, Lockheed Martin management maintained the 2026 guidance for diluted EPS of $29.35 to $30.25 and sales of $77.5 billion to $80.0 billion.

Importantly, also free cash flow guidance of $6.5 billion to $6.8 billion (not adjusted for stock-based compensation) was maintained, so I wouldn't overinterpret the negative free cash flow reported for Q1 2026.

This was largely due to working capital effects: A significant increase in contract assets (negative cash flow effect of $2.9 billion) and a modest decrease in contract liabilities (negative cash flow effect of $705 million) was only partially offset by favorable movements in accounts receivable and accounts payable.

All in all, operating cash flow in Q1 2026 was down $1.2 billion year-over-year, and came in at only $220 million. During the quarter, Lockheed paid $816 million in dividends and retired $1.0 billion in long-term debt. As a result, the company's cash balance declined to $1.9 billion, so by more than 50%. However, I'd argue that this is still plenty for a company like Lockheed Martin – not only from the perspective of generally solid free cash flow (on an annual basis) – but also given its strong liquidity (see p. 77 f., 2025 10-K).

Net debt remained largely stable compared to 2025 year-end, at manageable $19 billion (Figure 3, blue bars). This translates to a leverage ratio of roughly 3x expected free cash flow for 2025, after having adjusted for stock-based compensation, or SBC. Calculating leverage based on 2025 EBITDA ($9.4 billion) translates to a similarly manageable ratio of 2.0x. These numbers compare quite favorably to RTX Corp.'s (RTX) leverage (see my article published yesterday).

On a side note, the red bars in Figure 3 include unfunded pension liabilities, which have played a fairly significant role at Lockheed Martin in the past. However, as I detailed in some of my previous articles, the company continues to do a great job of reducing these liabilities. Compared to my last update, unfunded retirement obligations have remained stable and currently account for 7% and 9% of total and tangible assets, respectively.

Returning to Lockheed's operating fundamentals, and switching to a more long-term perspective, backlog was down 3.7% compared to 2025 year-end, but still substantial at $186 billion. Backlog continues to be well diversified, with a modest emphasis on Aeronautics (approximately 30% of total backlog).

The company has not yet released its 10-Q filing for the first quarter, which includes more in-depth information on backlog (which I usually cover in my articles). Hence, I cannot give an immediate update on timing-related aspects of backlog conversion. Nevertheless, I believe that the 2025 year-end statistics are still sufficiently up to date. In my view, the data illustrated in Figure 4 confirms that Lockheed Martin's sales visibility remains very solid. Backlog as of Q1 2026 end corresponds to three years of 2025 product sales – certainly plenty, but nonetheless quite a bit weaker than the backlog of RTX, which benefits from the continued strong demand in commercial aviation.

As for the company's longer-term earnings outlook, I would like to refer you to Figure 1 in my last article for reference purposes. Just about three months ago, analysts expected LMT's earnings to grow by 7.4% annually between 2026 and 2030. Note that this is the median figure, as the average would not be meaningful given the recovery expected in 2026 due to one-offs in 2025. Based on the current consensus, LMT is expected to grow its earnings per share by a median rate of 7.1% annually between 2026 and 2030, so insignificantly slower than previously expected (Figure 5). However, it is worth noting that the base (i.e., 2026 earnings) is now slightly higher, and earnings expectations are now less volatile. The expected EPS for 2030 is now $40, compared to about $37 when I wrote my last article.

Overall, it appears that analysts have already priced in a moderate earnings boost due to the escalation in Iran. However, the analyst consensus for 2026 largely aligns with Lockheed Martin management's guidance, which was issued back in January – so well before the start of the war in Iran. Irrespective of this, it is hard to deny that geopolitical tensions have increased in recent years, and I doubt that the situation will improve meaningfully going forward.

Against this backdrop, the upward revisions to long-term estimates are entirely understandable, and the geopolitical outlook remains one of the reasons why I currently have about 8% of my stock portfolio invested in defense-related companies. On a side note, I have illustrated my portfolio allocation in a separate article (see Figure 1 therein).

However, one aspect specific to Lockheed is certainly worth mentioning: In the Q4 and full-year 2025 earnings press release, management disclosed (not yet signed) seven-year framework agreements for the delivery of several missile types. I believe this is indeed an indication of a persistently solid demand environment, and the fact that the agreements cover multiple missile types (Advanced Patriot, Terminal High Altitude Area Defense - THAAD, and Precision Strike Missiles - PrSMs) and were recently signed further substantiates this view. Extrapolated to the broader portfolio and against the backdrop of the Acquisition Transformation Strategy recently announced by the U.S. Department of War, I believe that Lockheed's improved long-term earnings outlook is based on a solid foundation.

Of course, the tight fiscal situation of the U.S. could put a damper on this thesis, since the money to fund the continued growth of defense-related expenditures must come from somewhere, and foreign economies in particular are becoming increasingly reluctant to hold large amounts of U.S. Treasury securities (see this article for a more in-depth discussion).

Summary And Conclusion

Lockheed Martin reported generally rather disappointing results for the first quarter of 2026. While the company's backlog remains solid and confirms excellent revenue visibility, its performance is currently quite lackluster from both a top-line and an operational perspective.

While quarterly sales in the Space and Missiles and Fire Control segments were undoubtedly a bright spot, the broad-based decline in margins was discouraging. F-35 deliveries declined significantly compared to Q1 of 2025, and profit booking rate adjustments related to operational performance in the F-16 and C-130 programs were the main causes of a 1.3 percentage point decline in the Aeronautics segment margin. Profitability of the Space segment declined by a whopping 3.6 percentage points, which management attributed to an unfavorable prior-year comparison (along with volume increases and the resulting likely capacity constraints). However, I would argue that given the segment's typical profitability of around 10% (compared to just 8.2% in the first quarter of 2026), somewhat weak execution also played a role in the segment's quarterly performance.

Nevertheless, I found it reassuring that management reaffirmed its full-year guidance (importantly, including free cash flow guidance), suggesting that the current issues are only temporary. My long-term view on Lockheed Martin remains constructive from a geopolitical perspective and given the undeniably strong position of this defense-focused giant. However, I consider the selloff in response to the first-quarter results to be justified – despite the fact that LMT stock has already declined significantly from its all-time high of nearly $700, which was reached immediately after the war in Iran began.

In my view, the decline from the recent peak was healthy from a long-term perspective. At the time of writing, LMT stock is trading at around $530, and I believe this price reflects the fundamentals quite well. Compared to the significantly higher valuation of RTX (Figure 6 in yesterday's update), LMT stock is currently valued less optimistically, at an implied perpetual growth rate of 3% at a cost of equity of 8.0%, for example (Figure 6). Similarly, the 2026 forward price-to-earnings ratio of 18.6 is only slightly above the five-year average, and from a price-to-sales ratio perspective, LMT is trading in line with its historical average (see valuation dashboard).

Taken together, I believe it is appropriate to maintain a Hold rating on LMT stock. If the stock were to fall to its 2025 lows, I wouldn't mind adding a few shares here and there, as I don't believe the first-quarter 2026 report has called the long-term investment outlook into question.

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.

data.title: Why The Oldest Small-Cap CEF Is A Cornerstone Of My Portfolio - RVT
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

One of our core investing themes coming into 2026 was bullishness on small-cap stocks versus large-cap stocks.

In January, we noted that the S&P 500, which is weighted by market-cap, had been outperforming equal-weighted indexes in recent years. Historically, the equal-weighted index has outperformed over the long run. I wrote:

"There is a bit of an aura of invincibility surrounding large caps today. Will it fall apart in 2026? Not necessarily. But the outperformance of large caps is something that historically proved unsustainable.

I'm not guaranteeing that it will happen this year. What I am saying is that the huge gap between large-cap and small/mid-cap valuations should have investors thinking about what they are buying."

So far in 2026, small-caps have been outperforming, and the S&P 500 equal-weighted index has been outperforming the S&P 500 weighted by market-cap. The result is that the picks we suggested in that prior article are having a strong year:

Today, we want to zoom in on Royce Small-Cap Trust (RVT), yielding 6.5%.

RVT's Track Record

RVT is the oldest CEF in our portfolio, with an IPO in 1986. RVT holds the impressive track record of outperforming its index since inception on both a NAV and market price basis: Source

In particular, RVT performed exceptionally well relative to the S&P 500 through the Dot-Com bust, which was a period of time where:

  • Large-cap valuations were materially higher than small-cap valuations.
  • The S&P 500 had outperformed the S&P 500 Equal Weighted for several prior years.
  • Interest rates were high.
  • Stock market enthusiasm was strongly centered on a new technology.
  • Oil prices were spiking due to a war in the Middle East.

History might not repeat, but the rhyming is quite strong. Against that backdrop, here is how RVT performed:

While there are certainly some differences that investors can quibble about, the broad strokes are similar.

The Retirement of Royce

It is worth pointing out that founder and CEO Chuck Royce has stepped down from day-to-day management. However, the team managing the fund have all worked for Royce for 11 to 32 years. Apparently, in addition to being a good fund manager, Chuck is also a good boss to work for.

So while the management fund has changed with Royce stepping down, we continue to have confidence in a management team that has worked for and learned from him for decades.

RVT's Portfolio

RVT's largest sector exposures are industrials and financials, both of which are overweight relative to the index. Source

RVT's style is "small-cap core" with a diverse array of holdings in the small-cap universe.

RVT's Fee Structure

RVT has a performance fee structure that is based on the trailing 60-month performance relative to the index. If RVT outperforms over 60 months, the incentive fee is increased; if it underperforms, the fee is decreased by up to 0.50% on a base management fee of 1.00%.

If the total return is negative over the trailing 36 months, there is no management fee owed at all until performance turns positive. We've seen other managers willing to "waive" fees when performance is down, but for RVT, it is automatic and written in the management agreement. This creates a very shareholder-friendly dynamic where management is highly incentivized to beat the index and to have a positive total return.

RVT's Distribution

RVT also has a very unique distribution calculation. RVT takes the rolling average of the prior 4 quarter-end NAVs, and pays 1.75% of that per quarter. This is quite conservative and is less volatile than many CEFs that pay a variable dividend because RVT pays based on the average over 4 quarters, so even a particularly large swing in a single quarter has only a moderate impact on the distribution.

Where RVT sometimes has a significantly larger distribution is in Q4. The 7% annualized distributions made throughout the year is often not enough to cover taxable income during good years. So, RVT will frequently make a capital gains distribution in Q4, leading to oversized dividends for one quarter.

Excluding the Q4 oversized distribution, RVT's dividend has varied only about 7% from its bottom of $0.29 and the $0.31 distribution we project it will pay in June.

For our members at High Dividend Opportunities, we provide a variable dividend tracker where we calculate and project variable dividends for RVT as well as other variable dividend funds we hold. We expect the next distribution to be $0.31, and with NAV continuing to climb, reaching $0.32 this year is certainly a possibility.

The bottom line is that the dividend does vary, but it tends to change slowly because of how it is averaged over the trailing four quarters.

We are very bullish on RVT right now because we believe that small to mid-caps are undervalued relative to large-caps.

Conclusion

There are no guarantees in the stock market. Just because large-caps are trading at high valuations and have been outperforming, is far from a guarantee that the market will correct. Since RVT's distribution is tied to NAV, if NAV declines, the dividend will decline as well. Therefore, we suggest pairing RVT with other holdings that have fixed cash flow, like fixed-rate preferred equities and bonds.

The momentum so far in 2026 is positive, and we've seen small-caps outperforming large-caps. We believe that is going to continue as investors become more conscious of the price they are paying and the valuation of their investments.

RVT is a fund that has a proven track record going back nearly four decades, an experienced management team, shareholder-friendly terms, and a variable distribution policy that ensures a fair dividend that changes slowly over time.

We remain very bullish on RVT and consider it a cornerstone of our income portfolio.
